title: Shipfix-Global Market Update
summary:
Main
The BDI retreated for a fourth consecutive session on Tuesday, with lower demand for the Capesizes continuing to provide the headwinds. Among the commodities, energy prices retreated while more support for the Chinese economy contributed to higher iron ore and base metal prices. By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
Yesterday turned out to be quite an eventful day in China.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The BDI retreated for a fourth consecutive session on Tuesday, with lower demand for the Capesizes continuing to provide the headwinds. Among the commodities, energy prices retreated while more support for the Chinese economy contributed to higher iron ore and base metal prices.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: Yesterday turned out to be quite an eventful day in China. The country's leadership made the rare move to adjust the current year's budget.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: Crude oil extended Monday's losses during yesterday's session amid signs that the conflict between Israel and Hamas is being contained. The December Brent futures declined by 2.0 per cent, settling just above 88 dollars per barrel.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: The Baltic Dry Index extended its losing streak into a fourth consecutive session on Tuesday, with a decline of 3.4 per cent. Continued weakness for the Capesizes was the primary driver for the decline, but the Supramaxes and Handysizes also had a day in the red.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: As highlighted in previous editions of The Fix, the memories of the Chinese unofficial ban on imports of coal from Australia are fading fast. Since the beginning of the year, volumes have steadily recovered from virtually zero over the preceding two years, as China's appetite for the dirtiest of fossil fuels remains strong.
